I also took care of the visualization aspect of year over year percent change of foreign arrivals by ports of entry. The top 10 most visited ports of entry in the United States were selected for the purpose of data visualization. The dataset was a subset of the ‘Final COR Port of Entry’ provided by Fidelity. It was cleaned and converted into a dataframe using pandas in Python. Then, I used Plotly to turn the percent changes into bar chart. Finally, the codes were integrated into dash for the dashboard.

I scraped 20 years of air carrier statistics from the Bureau of Transportation Statistics. Once these datasets were downloaded, I cleaned each in R to select for only flights that originated in China or Hong Kong and arrived in the United States. These datasets were transformed into a more workable configuration in and flights with fewer than one passenger were dropped. I then ran a for loop that summed the total number of passengers coming in to the United States from Hong Kong and China per month for each year of data and added them to a new dataframe. These 20 dataframes were combined and a time series plot was created using plotly for Python.

I designed and implemented the multi-page dashboard using Dash and python, and then ported all the Plotly graphs into our final product, which I hosted locally during the datathon. I also integrated the News API to incorporate sources of alternative data into our dashboard. Finally, after the hackathon, I deployed our application used Heroku, so you can visit the dashboard from your own device!
